Subsequently this “napsterization” model has been applied in several Thiel-linked companies, including Facebook itself via its attempts to launch its Libra stablecoin, as detailed previously by Unlimited Hangout.
For example, Facebook itself faced many legal battles during its formative years, and thus employed many heavy hitters with ties to the public sector as legal counsel, including former State department official Jennifer Newstead, who had previously helped draft the Patriot Act. Another Facebook lawyer, Ted Ullyot, had worked in George W. Bush’s White House before Facebook.
Ullyot himself was suggested to Zuckerberg by Paul Cappuccio, a lawyer who had successfully represented AOL in landmark litigation regarding EU approval –– an outcome that helped globally enshrine American-made interests during the Internet’s proliferation. Both Cappuccio and Ullyot worked at Kirkland & Ellis, the law firm hired to defend Jeffrey Epstein, while Cappuccio helped Parker and Thiel deregulate and disrupt the electronic cigarette market as the general counsel for the now-bankrupt NJOY –– the first eVape company to receive FDA approval.
This “napsterization” model is evident in Coplan’s comments about TokenUnion, which he once referred to as an “economic experiment” that “could ultimately prove to be an integral part and one of the leading methods of storing value in the token ecosystem of tomorrow.” It is certainly interesting that Coplan would be able to attract millions of dollars in seed funding from figures such Lou Kerner and become deeply partnered with Bancor for something that was seen as an “economic experiment” and not a viable, for-profit company. That is, of course, unless the goal of both Coplan and his backers at TokenUnion was economic experimentation with the goal of “napsterizing” major aspects of this industry with a focus on the Ethereum ecosystem. Notably, in a recent media profile, Coplan was asked if he viewed Polymarket as a “technological descendant” of Napster, to which Coplan said “there are parallels.”
While Coplan was building the Ethereum-focused TokenUnion, the Ethereum-based prediction market platform Augur was taking off. Advised by Ethereum co-founder Vitalk Buterin and Robin Hanson and financed in part by a Thiel fellowship, Augur eventually “failed” to succeed in creating a globally successful prediction market. Developed and maintained by the Forecast Foundation non-profit, Augur had hinted–– before it became largely relevant and Krug left the foundation –– that it would offer a “widely anticipated for-profit spin-off.” Shortly after Krug stopped working on Augur, he joined Sean Parker at Thiel’s Founders Fund.
In keeping with the “napsterization” model, Krug and Founders Fund, with lessons learned from Augur, would become early investors and advisors to the for-profit Polymarket. Krug has since commented on the “symmetry” between the evolution of Napster into Spotify, via Sean Parker at Founders Fund, and the evolution of Augur into Polymarket, via Krug’s role at Founders Fund.
Joey Krug: Augur, Ethereum and Market Force Divination
Joey Krug, born in 1995, began mining Bitcoin in his bedroom in May 2011 ––at around 15 years old. By late 2013, Krug was inspired by a recent price surge to pursue cryptocurrency more seriously, and began a bitcoin club at his university, eventually founding a bitcoin point-of-sale company. In 2014, Krug teamed up with his college friend Jeremy Gardner and a computer scientist named Jack Peterson to put together a decentralized prediction market called Augur. Augur was inspired and derived from a Bitcoin-based project called TruthCoin. The first version of the white paper for TruthCoin was published in December 2013 by Paul Sztorc.
Interestingly, only three years before the renewed interest in cryptocurrency-based prediction markets, DARPA’s sister agency — the Intelligence Advanced Research Projects Activity (IARPA), which is responsible for “pay[ing] for experimental projects for the U.S. intelligence community” — announced they were launching “a four-year, $50 million program” that would pay people willing to predict major world events. This program, known as Aggregative Contingent Estimation, or ACE, was headed by Jason Matheny. After running ACE and, later, IARPA as a whole, Matheny launched the Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET) with a $55 million grant he received from the Open Philanthropy Project –– co-founded by Facebook co-founder Dustin Moskovitz. The ACE program officially ended in June 2015, shortly after Krug formalized the non-profit entity tied to Augur development –– the Forecast Foundation.
Soon after the launch of the Ethereum network in 2014, Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin approached Krug. Buterin had recently received a Thiel fellowship to further the development of Ethereum. Buterin convinced Krug to develop what would become the prediction market Augur, not on Bitcoin as originally planned, but on Ethereum. Buterin argued that doing so would allow Krug to take advantage of a more expressive smart contract programming language than what was then available on Bitcoin.
Krug and his team, now counting Buterin as an advisor, then founded the Forecast Foundation in Estonia in December 2014 as a non-profit entity meant to further develop the Augur platform. Another advisor, Ron Bernstein, was the co-founder of InTrade, which previously had partnered with DARPA to provide data for their PAM experiments. Bernstein’s Tradesports.com, which had acquired InTrade in 2003, had leased the technology to the Forecast Foundation behind Augur in exchange for REP tokens created during their groundbreaking fundraiser –– leading Tradesports.com to amass a large ETH stake after it converted its REP token holdings into ETH.
Interestingly, during this same time, Augur co-founder Jeremy Gardner posted in 2015 that some on the Augur team were “speaking to a few several-letter agencies” to “help them understand the technology we’re building.” Gardner went so far as to say that “the DoD, DARPA, and CIA are huge fans of Augur” and the prediction market platform it sought to build. Upon leaving Augur in a messy lawsuit, Gardner joined Blockchain Capital –– an influential cryptocurrency venture firm founded by Tether co-founder Brock Pierce, which features prominently in The Chain series.
Notably, the Augur team soon added the architect of DARPA’s failed PAM prediction market, Robin Hanson, as an advisor. The Augur logo notably bears significant similarities to the logos of DARPA’s failed TIA and PAM projects (i.e. a “one-eyed” pyramid). In fact, a profile on Augur from 2018 notes that Krug, one of his co-founders, and two unnamed Augur advisors (one of which was likely Hanson) had explicitly decided to make the Augur logo “a pyramid, with three points converging upon an all-seeing eye.” This, among other similarities, suggests that Augur was an effort to resurrect PAM, particularly given that, in a few short years, Thiel’s tentacles would extend deeply into Augur and were already present, indirectly, via Buterin’s Thiel fellowship.
In the fall of 2015, the Forecast Foundation and Augur operated the first ever initial coin offering (ICO) on the Ethereum blockchain with their token REP. Reports from October 2015, during its ICO, claim that Augur was courting “four major banks,” in addition to a partnership with IBM Watson in efforts to develop a “closed-source alternative” to Augur. This fundraising method, analogous to the initial public offering (IPO), came to dominate the remainder of the decade in the cryptocurrency space –– leading its pioneer Krug to eventually join two of the most influential investing firms in the industry, Pantera Capital and Peter Thiel’s Founders Fund. In June 2016, Krug himself was awarded a Thiel Fellowship to further develop Augur. The next year, he joined Dan Morehead’s Pantera Capital as a co-chief investor officer (CIO) in 2017.
In a June 2017 post published on his Medium announcing his Pantera appointment, Krug specifically noted that this would help solve a dire need for liquidity on Augur, even stating that Pantera would be actively trading on it:
Once Augur is out, we can even trade on it, which will increase liquidity and be good for everyone. There are no set allocations for anything in the fund, it is all about capacity and what we can spend the money on that is the best investment/most profitable.
If a million people show up day one of Augur’s full release, then there is probably no need (or edge) for us to trade. But if it does not happen, we will trade as long as we have an edge. This is not altruism: we are not planning to lose money to attract users to Augur! However, since we have an edge even in markets like bitcoin, we will almost certainly have an edge on Augur markets for quite a while.
In July 2018, Augur released its primordial iteration based upon the native Ethereum network token, ETH, and its REP token that had been created during its groundbreaking ICO. According to Krug, version one of Augur was “a very slow, expensive, difficult to use version” and left users exposed to varying price fluctuations of tokens. As the Augur blog noted in 2021, version one was “the first real world deployment of a free and open prediction market,” which “allowed the community to test many hypotheses and ideas that had been written about for decades prior by many academics, software engineers and free-market information enthusiasts.” In other words, it was an economic experiment.
Version two of Augur, launched in 2020, replaced the unit of account with a stablecoin, specifically using DAI –– an algorithmic stablecoin managed by MakerDAO. Despite version two solving a lot of issues from version one, it “became clear that Augur was too resource-intensive to live fully on Ethereum mainnet.” Due to this and because of the evolution of externally-traded Augur shares on existing automated markets, the Forecast Foundation was “ultimately” led to launch Augur Turbo in 2021. Augur Turbo partnered with oracle provider Chainlink and Ethereum Layer 2 (L2) Polygon –– both infrastructure partners that Polymarket would utilize only a few years later.
These decisions to move towards L2s and stablecoins were anything but trivial. In fact, Krug himself has expressed that, without cryptocurrency, prediction markets would be unable to function. According to Krug, two of the main pillars of the prediction market industry at large –– a 24/7 global, internet-based market and (practically) irreversible transactions –– helped “unlock” prediction markets’ ability to function and succeed.
In a traditional online betting market, with legacy financial rails such as credit cards, there are few legal protections for book makers to successfully fend off scams in the event of a market loser claiming that their losing bet was made fraudulently. The credit card company ultimately becomes the oracle of the pay-out, meaning that it solely holds the decision to settle the funding transaction even after the event the bet is predicated on has passed. Cryptocurrency also helps market makers circumnavigate laws such as the Unlawful Internet Gambling Enforcement Act, which prohibits “gambling businesses from knowingly accepting payments in connection with the participation of another person in a bet or wager that involves the use of the Internet.”
For example, a user could bet $100 that the United States will win the World Cup, and even after the team is eliminated and the terms of the bet are closed, said user could phone their credit card company and claim the bet was made fraudulently and ask for their funds to be returned. In contrast, when a bet such as this is made using stablecoins in the form of a smart contract, even prior to completion of the terms of the bet, the payouts are already funded and set to be distributed. Unfortunately, the use of cryptocurrency alone does not completely remove the need for trust in such a scenario, with a smart contract innately requiring an oracle to feed the correct market outcome data, which then triggers the payouts. This issue, known colloquially as the oracle problem, and how it relates to Polymarket’s legal standing and centralization is to be discussed in greater detail in Part II of this investigation.
While still at Augur, Krug received an email from Shayne Coplan describing ideas to improve Augur, and sent further repeated investment pitches for Pantera’s then-CIO to consider. Krug explained that “prediction markets are one of the hardest types of start-ups to build,” and that while “there have been dozens… none of them hit escape velocity.” As noted earliuer in this piece, the Augur co-founder told Coplan that if he could create a prediction market that reached a target “volume of $5 million a week,” then he would invest.
During and after his time at Augur, Krug participated in many advisory roles within the cryptocurrency industry, notably including a 2019 appointment to the advisory board of a novel dollar stablecoin project called Ampleforth. Ampleforth was created to develop a dollar stablecoin that fluctuates its supply based on external information, like the consumer price index (CPI), in order to stabilize purchasing power.
This project brought Krug’s expertise in cryptocurrency-based prediction markets alongside giants of the retail-service data industry, including former employees of Google, Uber and Facebook –– all companies that have notably pioneered data-driven “predictions” as part of their commercial products. For example, one former Uber employee who joined Ampleforth, Aditya Sarawgi, worked on “Uber Places” which necessitated “auto-complete search, destination prediction and current location prediction,” and became “the primary engineering point” during a “multi year maps contract between Google and Uber.”
Uber was notorious for hiring former intelligence officers, including former CIA, FBI and NSA employees, and it reportedly “amassed a high-functioning corporate espionage force.” Its founder, Travis Kalanick, was a fellow peer-to-peer data pioneer, having developed a program similar to Napster called Scour before he led a hostile, technology-driven takeover of the world’s taxi industry. As noted earlier, Kalanick was the other tech billionaire that Coplan closely studied, in addition to Mark Zuckerberg, and was the other figure alongside the Facebook founder that Coplan has acknowledged he sought to emulate.
Kalanick and his company’s utter dismissal of the current legislative environment while building the disruptive ride sharing business would go on to greatly influence Coplan’s indifference to the law (i.e. Coplan’s “act first, apologize later” model) while Polymarket was breaking ground during (and before) the 2024 election cycle. The similarities between Napster, Facebook, Uber and Polymarket include their legal disruption-as-a-service business models and their formative operations within a regulatory gray-area –– if not simply illegal. Other areas of overlap are discussed below and in Part II.
Alongside Krug on Ampleforth’s board sat members of the Hoover Institute, an eventual technical project manager of Facebook’s stablecoin project Libra, and an early, influential Facebook employee Sam Lessin. Lessin was a former classmate of Zuckerberg’s at Harvard, who sold his company to Facebook and formally joined the company in 2010. Lessin also played a crucial role during the company’s founding as his father, Wall Street banker Robert Lessin, “took Zuckerberg around New York to meet with venture capitalists and executives in the finance and media industries” after their spring semester in 2004.
Krug, having joined Thiel’s Founders Fund in April 2023 to formally partner with both Thiel and Sean Parker to help shape “the next decade of Founders Fund’s crypto strategy,” promptly led a $45 million investment round in Polymarket. The investment was announced in 2024, shortly after Coplan met the fabled “$5 million a week” mark.
In a December 2025 interview, Krug described Augur as failing while Polymarket has succeeded mainly because “the timing was just wrong” and “we were way too early.” Krug furthered that “hopefully we inspired some people to build in the space,” and that “maybe Augur is Napster and Polymarket is Spotify.”
The Napsterization of Everything
The story has been carefully crafted over the past few years that a young college drop-out named Shayne Coplan, after being inspired by Austrian economists and Robin Hanson, founded Polymarket in mid-2020 completely alone and then “built his company in his bathroom.” As detailed extensively in this article, there is plenty of background information and key data points that have been clearly left out of this story in order for this particular narrative of Shayne Coplan’s and Polymarket’s origins to take hold.
This narrative bears many similarities to the stories that have been woven regarding the origins of major Big Tech firms, whose founders Coplan explicitly hoped to emulate. In many of those cases, these founders are said to have built their companies “in their garage” or something similarly humble, only for it to trickle out years later that their companies’ success stories were only made possible by funding provided by U.S. intelligence or similarly shady organization and groups.
In the case of Coplan, despite efforts made to obfuscate his past, he was clearly immersed in networks within the cryptocurrency agency with ties to Israel and Israeli intelligence well before he founded Polymarket. During this time, he talked about the “napsterization of finance,” only to have the venture capital firm of Napster’s Sean Parker and crypto prediction market pioneer Joey Krug be among the first backers of Polymarket. Krug claims that Polymarket was successful where others were not because of its preferable “user interface.” In reality, it was Coplan’s connections to the networks he developed while running TokenUnion combined with his careful study and emulation of Sean Parker of Napster, Travis Kalanick of Uber and Mark Zuckerberg of Facebook that made Coplan and his company a success.
If there is a pattern emerging from this disruption nexus of prediction market makers, peer-to-peer data pioneers, and social network founders, perhaps it is the brazen disinterest in upholding the law during their respective incubation and early growth periods. Due to the proximity of these businesses to the ideas that stemmed from the likes of DARPA and the U.S. intelligence community, it is arguable that these companies were silently given a free pass to build where others could not, and quickly gained an insurmountable market share from being the first movers in an otherwise illegal market. But regardless of a hypothetical, State-sponsored get-out-of-jail-free card, these generations of Silicon Valley’s best and brightest quite literally changed the limits of their industry by changing the rules of their regulators.
Parker’s Napster was blatantly illegal, as was Kalanick’s Uber, and yet both models survived in their own way –– Uber stopped paying fines on behalf of drivers and started lobbying local, state and federal government, while the Napster founder funded and advised Spotify into becoming the largest music streaming service in the world. A willingness to “break the rules” while building a company combined with teaming up with the “right” people, invariably tied to the military, intelligence or both, is the recipe that Coplan studied and then followed in building Polymarket.
As both Krug and Coplan have alluded, Polymarket’s path seems closer to the Napster-into-Spotify model, in which Augur built the infrastructure and demonstrated the market demand for the product that Polymarket is now delivering. The similarities between the companies of this nexus are immense; young founders were raided by the FBI; the companies had employees who boasted working –– likely collaborating –– with the intelligence community; they started as companies that potentially offered illegal services; they were advised and funded by the Thiel, Conway and Pincus network at a young age; they rode waves of political favors and regulatory tide shifts towards eventual legitimacy; and finally, many of them seem to be awfully similar to the same family of controversial projects once housed at DARPA.
Another argument to be made is that the government –– whether demonstrated by actions of various legislative agencies, court systems and law enforcement –– was only feigning resistance to these founders and companies. Under the cover of fines, raids and court summons, these intelligence and military-backed technology companies were painted as outsiders fighting against the legacy systems, while in actuality pursuing the very interests of the Pentagon and the intelligence community. Indeed, as this article makes clear, the military and intelligence community have long been fans of prediction markets, whether PAM, Augur, or now, Polymarket.
With Polymarket’s odds now capable of moving the stock market, manipulating the media and even influencing elections, the real architects of the explosion in prediction markets have a new tool that allows them not only unprecedented predictive power, but also unprecedented abilities to insider trade while they seed select narratives and alter our digital reality.
As will be explored in Part II, the fact that both Joey Krug and Shayne Coplan were inspired specifically by Robin Hanson’s work on futarchy, which proposes using prediction markets as a system of public governance, is highly significant. Perhaps even more significant is the fact futarchy is already taking hold in significant ways under the current administration, a turn of events that is particularly jarring considering that the President’s son is both invested in and advising Polymarket.
As broached here but explored in greater detail in the next part of this investigation, Polymarket’s success heralds a new era for both finance and for governance –– one where the house always wins.
articles.summary:
Polymarket founder Shayne Coplan and industry peers are facing scrutiny over the platform's origins, alleged ties to intelligence-linked networks, and the potential for market manipulation in political betting.

### Key Points
- Polymarket and competitor Kalshi have emerged as dominant prediction markets, often operating with minimal regulatory oversight despite concerns regarding insider trading.
- Shayne Coplan’s rise is linked to a network of Silicon Valley figures, including Peter Thiel and Sean Parker, who have historically backed ventures with intelligence-community ties.
- The platform’s development mirrors the "napsterization" model, where companies intentionally operate in regulatory gray areas to gain market share before seeking legitimacy.
- Critics argue Polymarket resurrects concepts from the defunct Pentagon program Policy Analysis Market (PAM), which was originally designed to predict geopolitical instability and terror attacks.
- High-profile media partnerships and the involvement of political figures, such as Donald Trump Jr., have raised significant questions about conflicts of interest and the integrity of market data.

### Why it Matters
The rapid normalization of prediction markets as tools for political and financial forecasting grants private entities unprecedented influence over public perception and governance. This shift toward "futarchy" risks replacing traditional democratic processes with market-based systems that may be susceptible to manipulation by well-connected insiders.

articles.title: Can't afford a high-powered graphics card? This DIY engineer made his own GPU out of 8,192 RISC-V chips
articles.author: Christian Cawley
articles.content:
- YouTuber bitluni has unveiled a RISC-V DIY Graphics Processing Unit
- Builder has a library of insane DIY projects on his YouTube channel
- RISC-V microcontrollers were sourced from AliExpress
YouTuber bitluni has unveiled his latest project, which might put any concerns you have over GPU pricing into context. The German DIYer, who has built up a strong following with his selection of intriguing and off-the-wall projects, has assembled a GPU using a large collection of affordable RISC-V microcontrollers (MCUs).
While the results are impressive for a DIY build, the GPU is unfortunately quite modest, and would probably have been abandoned long before now had a PCB design firm not got in touch to explore a partnership over the build.
Demonstrating the project on his channel, bitluni – real name Matthias Balwierz – expressed how difficult the build was, and how it almost drove him mad.
8000+ RISC-V MCUs output at 320x200 resolution
In his latest video, bitluni recalls a previous attempt at building a GPU in late 2025 which lead to PCB designers Altium getting involved, explaining, “The clusters I made before were already challenging my sanity. I thought I was done with the topic, but the budget and these tools would allow for a cluster of a different magnitude, and the magnitude I had in mind was just insane.”
Pushing things to the next level is a hallmark of bitluni’s videos, and the finished build has an incredible 8,192 RISC-V CH570 microcontrollers, each running at 100 MHz, with 12KB of SRAM. These are mounted on blades, which went through several revisions as the PCB company declared them “too complicated.”
Each CH570 has an array of LEDs mounted, and these correspond to each MCU, and the equivalent QVGA pixel. With 8,192 MCUs, the resulting 320x200 might seem modest, but bitluni already has plans for a more power version with 32,000 MCUs.
Graphics or hashing?
The spike in GPU prices before the AI boom was thanks to industrial-scale cryptocurrency hashing, which graphics cards a particularly suited to. It is this relationship between hashing and graphics that gives bitluni’s project some hope beyond displaying images and video.
Sign up to the TechRadar Pro newsletter to get all the top news, opinion, features and guidance your business needs to succeed!
Identifying the serial port as the bottleneck to the project’s success as a working (if unwieldy) GPU, bitluni has appealed for help from his community of viewers to find a workaround, but that didn’t bring the project to a close.
Changing tack, he used the microcontrollers for hashing and found it outperformed his PC’s 8 core CPU, with a low 4 watt power draw, so perhaps there is a use for this approach after all.
Follow TechRadar on Google News and add us as a preferred source to get our expert news, reviews, and opinion in your feeds.
Christian Cawley has extensive experience as a writer and editor in consumer electronics, IT and entertainment media. He has contributed to TechRadar since 2017 and has been published in Computer Weekly, Linux Format, ComputerActive, and other publications.
He currently heads up the team at smart home website Matter Alpha, and writes about retro gaming at Gaming Retro.
